title: Mixture-of-Transformers Learn Faster: A Theoretical Study on Classification Problems
abstract: This paper introduces the Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) model, where each transformer block acts as a distinct expert with its own attention and FFN layers, with a gating network that dynamically assigns data to specialized experts. The paper provides the first theoretical analysis with benefit characterization for full-transformer specialization, studying how attention- and FFN-level specialization interact and how such architectures converge during training.

methodology.architecture: Each transformer block has its own attention and FFN layers with a gating network for data assignment
methodology.training_process: Three-stage training algorithm with continuous gating updates
stages: Stage 1: Freeze self-attention, train FFNs to encourage diversity
Stage 2: Freeze FFNs, train attention layers
Stage 3: Fine-tune FFNs to reinforce specialization

results.specialization: Each transformer expert specializes in a distinct class of tasks
results.routing: Gating network accurately routes data samples to the correct expert
results.attention_benefit: Self-attention reduces training loss by extracting relevant classification signals
results.convergence: Expected prediction loss converges to within ϵ-accuracy in 𝒪(log(ϵ⁻¹)) iterations via gradient descent
results.improvement: Significantly shortens convergence time from 𝒪(ϵ⁻¹) to 𝒪(log(ϵ⁻¹)) compared to existing multi-head transformers

contributions: First theoretical analysis with benefit characterization for full-transformer specialization
Three-stage training algorithm with continuous gating updates
Theoretical guarantees on convergence of MoT architecture to optimum
Demonstration of MoT's advantage over attention-absent MoE models
